segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a vibrant Gucci advertisement featuring a model in a bold blue and red ensemble, accessorized with a chain-strap bag, set against a tropical backdrop with the 'GUCCI' logo prominently displayed, as gentle, atmospheric music begins and a female voice declares, "The story at the heart of Gucci is extraordinary." The scene transitions to a black-and-white archival clip of a woman in a striped dress dancing energetically in a sunlit outdoor setting, while a male voice adds, "There are a lot of rumors around." This is followed by a close-up of a woman in a dark top with a serious expression, as the female voice continues to describe the brand's narrative, saying, "It has got passions, it's got family, it's got feuding, it's got loves and loathings and tragedy, even murder." The narrative shifts to a fashion show where models in avant-garde outfits, including one with a blue furry mask and another in a white hooded outfit with oversized sunglasses, walk past an audience, while a male voice with a distinct accent philosophizes, "It's better to cry in Rolls-Royce than to be happy in on a bicycle." A close-up of a woman in a brown leather jacket appears, followed by a black-and-white scene of a woman in a white dress walking through a crowded, ornate space, as the initial female voice returns to emphasize, "That story is really the appeal and the excitement and yes, the sex factor of a brand like Gucci." The video then cuts to a woman in a bright green jacket walking in a dark environment, followed by a woman in a black jacket sitting at a table in a well-lit room with a window behind her, while the atmospheric music continues and a male voice reflects, "Sometimes things happen in your life that change your life." The scene changes to a nighttime cityscape with silhouettes of people on a rooftop overlooking a river, then to a magazine cover titled 'STYLE WARS' featuring two men in suits, as the male voice continues, "This was really success beyond my wildest imagination." A close-up of an older man with gray hair and a beard appears, followed by a man in a suit walking down a runway as the audience applauds. The video returns to the older man, now in a different setting, as the male voice continues to express disbelief, stating, "I didn't believe it possible. Tom did not believe it was possible. The success was unbelievable." The video then shows a woman in a floral dress with dark nail polish, touching her hair. The final frames show a Gucci advertisement with a model in a blue top and red skirt, followed by a close-up of a woman in a patterned outfit with colorful beads, and ends with the woman in the black jacket at the table, smiling slightly, while the female voice concludes, "It's difficult to understate just how successful the Gucci brand has been in history but also really is right now. We're looking at a brand value of something like..." before fading out.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a close-up of a woman's eye overlaid with a social media interface, as a female voice discusses Gucci's "15 billion dollars" and its "extraordinary social media following." The visuals quickly transition to a model in a bright yellow 'NY' hoodie before showing a man in a blue Gucci tracksuit with a crocodile-skin handbag. The female voice continues as a woman in a black zippered jacket is seen gesturing at a table, explaining that Gucci's "business value" and "dynamism in the digital world" have been "the key to Gucci's success." A close-up of a Gucci-branded object is followed by a book being flipped open to reveal vintage Gucci imagery, including a black-and-white photo of a store and a colorful fashion spread. An older man with gray hair and tortoiseshell glasses appears, speaking thoughtfully, with archival photos of men in suits and a woman in a white dress overlaid, while the female voice concludes her statement. A text overlay quotes Madonna, emphasizing Gucci's cultural impact, before a title card for 'Bloomberg Businessweek Presents Storylines: The Real House of Gucci' appears. The scene shifts to a scenic shot of a river in Florence with a waterfall and historic buildings, as a male voice with a distinct accent begins to narrate, lamenting an "unfairness" in giving credit and that people who deserved it were "totally forgotten," asserting that "nobody has focused on the real story" but rather on the "fairy tale," which, while "good news for the viewers," is "not the reality." The final visual features Sara Forden, Editor of Bloomberg News and author of 'The House of Gucci,' speaking expressively, and her voice is heard at the end of the segment, mentioning an "epic saying in Italy" concerning "family generations."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Sara Forden, a woman with short red hair and glasses, wearing a black sweater over a white collared shirt, seated in a well-lit room with a fireplace and bookshelf behind her. She speaks expressively, using hand gestures, introducing the concept that the "first generation creates, second generation expands, and the third generation destroys," noting this is "almost what happened in the Gucci story." A subtle, reflective instrumental background music begins to play. The scene transitions to a black-and-white archival photo of Guccio Gucci, labeled with his name, as Sara identifies him as "the founder" who "had the vision for making quality leather goods" and selling them "initially to American tourists and other European travelers." A timeline graphic indicating 'Gucci opens 1921' appears. The narrative continues with a family tree-style visual, introducing black-and-white portraits of Guccio's two sons, Aldo Gucci and Rodolfo Gucci, connected by lines, as Sara explains they each owned "50% of the company." She emphasizes that "especially Aldo was really the marketer and the expander," with a vision to take Gucci "outside of Italy." A group photo shows an older man, presumably Aldo Gucci, flanked by two women, set against a floral backdrop. The video then cuts to a vintage black-and-white street scene of a Gucci store on Fifth Ave., New York, with pedestrians and a hot dog cart visible, visually reinforcing the brand's expansion. Returning to Sara Forden, she continues her commentary, discussing the "third generation" where "a lot of the conflict started." The family tree expands to include Aldo's three sons: Georgio Gucci, Paolo Gucci, and Roberto Gucci, as Sara states they "had 50% together with their father." Finally, Rodolfo Gucci’s son, Maurizio Gucci, appears in a smiling black-and-white portrait with glasses, identified by Sara as "a single child who inherited 50% from his father." The visual style consistently uses black-and-white imagery and a dark background patterned with Gucci’s interlocking G logo, emphasizing the brand’s legacy and complex family dynamics throughout the segment.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a sepia-toned close-up of an open book displaying vintage Gucci designs and a black-and-white photograph of a Gucci store entrance with two smiling men, as soft, ambient background music begins. The camera zooms in on one of the men's faces before transitioning to Sara Forden, a woman with short red hair and glasses, wearing a black sweater over a white collared shirt, seated in a well-lit room with a fireplace and bookshelf behind her. She speaks expressively with hand gestures, narrating that 'The Gucci story was really punctuated by the family conflicts' and that they were 'extremely litigious,' leading to fights that 'spilled out... into open court and became extremely public.' A black-and-white archival photo then shows three men in suits with mannequins in avant-garde outfits, suggesting a fashion show or design studio. Sara Forden reappears, continuing her narration, identifying 'Domenico De Sole' as a key character who 'started as a lawyer to Rodolfo in the early 80s.' A family tree graphic appears, showing black-and-white portraits of Rodolfo Gucci and Domenico De Sole, with a connecting line labeled 'Domenico hired as lawyer 1980'. The scene shifts to a modern interview setting where Sara Forden, now in a black zippered jacket, sits across from an older man with graying hair and tortoiseshell glasses, wearing a dark suit and light blue shirt. Sara greets him, 'So hi Domenico,' and asks, 'So what was it like working for Rodolfo and then dealing with Aldo?' The older man, Domenico De Sole, responds thoughtfully, his voice describing the situation as 'very complicated' due to a 'big conflict in the family' because 'Aldo's children probably felt that their dad did such a phenomenal job.' The video cuts to a black-and-white archival shot of two men in a workshop, examining leather goods and handbags, with Gucci products displayed on a table. The segment concludes with a close-up of Domenico De Sole smiling warmly, followed by a color portrait of him laughing, resting his hand on his cheek, wearing a dark suit, light blue shirt, and a ring with a blue stone, set against a patterned backdrop, as the ambient music continues.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Domenico De Sole, former CEO of Gucci, an older man with graying hair and a beard, wearing a dark suit, speaking thoughtfully in a modern, well-lit room. He speaks with a calm, clear voice and a slight accent, discussing past 'battles' over share ownership and how 'Maurizio kept the 50%.' He is seen in conversation with a woman in a black zippered jacket. The scene then transitions to archival footage of two men in suits standing before mannequins dressed in avant-garde fashion, followed by a scenic shot of a river in Florence with a stone bridge and historic buildings. Archival footage of Paolo Gucci, an older man in a pinstripe suit, seated beside a woman in a green top, appears next. A close-up of Maurizio Gucci, a man with dark hair and large tortoiseshell glasses, shows him with a serious expression. A male voice with a distinct, slightly higher pitch and a clear accent describes him as someone who 'dreams every five minutes' and 'doesn't think, he dreams,' whose 'whole life it still ran as a child.' Another deeper, authoritative male voice with an accent then states business figures of '$138 million business with $18 million profit,' concluding, 'This is the answer to a dream. I accept.' The video then displays a family tree graphic with black-and-white portraits of Rodolfo Gucci and Maurizio Gucci, set against a dark background with a repeating Gucci logo pattern. A clear, articulate female voice with an American accent narrates a 'major turning point' in 1983, explaining that 'Rodolfo is very ill and he dies,' leaving his '50% stake in the company to his only son Maurizio.'

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Sara Forden, a woman with short red hair and glasses, wearing a black sweater over a white collared shirt, seated in a well-lit room with a fireplace and bookshelf behind her. She speaks expressively, gesturing with red-painted nails, her clear, articulate voice narrating how someone (implied Maurizio) wanted to 'clean up the Gucci brand and take it up market,' but couldn't get his uncle and cousins on board due to their lucrative licensing deals. As a vintage color photograph of four men in tuxedos, including Maurizio Gucci, appears, Sara Forden explicitly states, 'As Maurizio is becoming more and more convinced of his vision for Gucci and that he's the one who needs to take it forward.' This transitions to a photo of Maurizio Gucci and Patrizia Reggiani, elegantly dressed and smiling, as Sara Forden explains Maurizio was 'falling out with his wife Patrizia, who had been pushing him to be that kind of Mr. Gucci.' A black-and-white family tree graphic then appears, showing portraits of Maurizio and Patrizia with text indicating their 'split 1985,' which Sara Forden confirms, stating Maurizio 'decides to leave her.' The graphic updates to show Patrizia Reggiani’s name change to Patrizia Gucci, followed by a close-up archival clip from 1986 showing Patrizia in a brown leather jacket with dramatic makeup. During this, a distinct, slightly accented female voice interjects, 'We have this transformation. Mr. Jekyll became Dr. Hyde.' The scene continues with a photo of Paolo Gucci in a suit beside a classical statue, then Patrizia Gucci and Paolo Gucci at a social event, and concludes with a black-and-white portrait of an older man with glasses, identified as Aldo Gucci, smiling thoughtfully. Sara Forden's voice returns, stating that 'at the same time you had Paolo ratting on his father Aldo to the US.' Subtle, dramatic background music underscores the entire segment, becoming more prominent during transitions.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a blurry, dark night scene of a Japanese cityscape, filled with glowing neon signs and vertical lights, evoking a bustling urban atmosphere. This transitions into a vibrant, sunlit daytime street scene in Japan, crowded with pedestrians walking past colorful storefronts and towering signs with Japanese characters, including one for 'Inc.' During these scenes, Domenico De Sole, an older man with graying hair and a beard, wearing a dark suit and light blue shirt, speaks thoughtfully with a slight accent, explaining how "really geniuses" opened stores in places like "Japan, in Hong Kong, in Asia," and how "early on they were in all these markets." He continues, describing a problem where the product was "getting to be less attractive" and how expanded distribution led to the brand "deteriorating." The scene then cuts to a close-up of a woman in a light pink blouse and green jacket, smiling warmly as she walks through the Japanese crowd. The focus shifts to the interview setting where Domenico speaks, seated across from Sara Forden, a woman with short red hair and glasses, wearing a black leather jacket, who holds papers and listens attentively. As the audio transitions to the distinct sounds of birds chirping, the video shows a panoramic view of Florence, showcasing its historic architecture, the Arno River, and the iconic Duomo under a clear sky, followed by a close-up of a pigeon perched on a weathered building ledge. Sara Forden then speaks with a clear, articulate British accent, noting that "there are many lessons from luxury brand history about the balance between brand value and revenue growth." She warns that it's "easy to get over-excited about the possibility of revenue growth, increased distribution," and specifically mentions "Gucci" having "their own issues and challenges on losing control of the brand experience." Visually, the scene changes to a cobblestone street in Florence where people walk past outdoor market stalls displaying leather goods, including handbags and belts. A woman in a black jacket browses items, while another woman in a blue jacket examines a handbag. Sara concludes by stating that brands have "over-expanded" and "lost control of product quality," as the camera pans through a narrow market alley lined with colorful handbags and accessories, capturing the lively atmosphere, and ending with a close-up of a row of luxury handbags in various colors and textures, including crocodile and snake skin, displayed on a wooden shelf, emphasizing craftsmanship and design.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with an eerie, sustained background sound accompanying tense archival footage from 1995. A chaotic scene unfolds outside a stone building with arched doorways, where a crowd of reporters, photographers, and police officers, including Carabinieri, gather around a white police vehicle with blue flashing lights. A woman with short red hair and glasses, speaking in a calm English narration, sets the scene: "In March of 1995, one morning, broad daylight, Maurizio is walking into the front door of his new offices. A gunman comes into the doorway behind him and shoots him dead in cold blood." Amidst this, a body covered in a white sheet is carried on a stretcher by uniformed personnel. An unidentified male voice, speaking in Italian over the archival footage, adds a specific detail: "Quindi, lui viene colpito alla spalla" (So, he is hit in the shoulder). The scene shifts to an indoor interview with the woman, who speaks expressively, gesturing in a bright, cozy room with books. The video cuts back to the 1995 scene, showing the body being loaded into a gray van marked 'DOMENICO DE SOLE' and '438', still surrounded by police and media. On-screen text identifies police inspector Paolo La Forgia, stating, 'He was shot three times in the head - like an execution,' providing grim context. The woman in the interview continues her reflection, stating, "The murder of Maurizio Gucci was a shocking event for the city, and in addition to being shocking, it was very sinister because there was no clear culprit. The prosecutor who was investigating the case was looking into his business deals, he was looking into the family conflicts. He interviewed the cousins, he interviewed Patrizia, but he didn't really see a clear family connection." The sequence alternates between the somber, chaotic 1995 event and the calm, reflective interview, all underscored by the persistent eerie background sound.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a vintage color photograph of Maurizio Gucci and Patrizia Reggiani, elegantly dressed and smiling beside a stylized leopard backdrop, evoking a glamorous 1980s or 1990s social event. A deep, thoughtful male voice immediately begins, reflecting on "somebody who was killed by his wife," a clear reference to Maurizio. The scene transitions to a close-up of Paolo La Forgia, a police inspector, speaking with a serious expression in a softly lit room, his face marked by deep lines, with on-screen text stating, 'He was shot three times in the head - like an execution.' The male voice continues, asserting that "Maurizio's real legacy is that the Gucci brand has really been created by three generations," but notes that "after the third generation, the family is not able to keep control of the company." This narrative is visually supported by a black-and-white family tree graphic, tracing the Gucci lineage from Guccio Gucci through Aldo and Rodolfo to Maurizio, highlighting corporate shifts like Paolo Gucci’s shares going to Investcorp, depicted as a modern glass building. The male voice concludes, stating that "Maurizio not only lost control of the company, but he also lost his life," as the scene returns to a more intense Paolo La Forgia. An eerie, sustained drone sound then accompanies a poignant shot of Maurizio Gucci's snowy grave, marked 'MAURIZIO 1948' and 'GUCCI 1995', surrounded by snow-covered evergreen branches. The drone continues over a wide shot of Florence’s Neptune Fountain, with water spraying and a white van passing by. Finally, Rita Clifton, a brand expert, appears, speaking with a clear British accent about how "the founder, the visionary, the original creative director" of luxury brands often reach the "end of their tenure," necessitating "somebody else to take the brand on in a different direction or a fresh direction." The segment concludes with a close-up of an open book displaying vintage, sepia-toned Gucci imagery, including a black-and-white photo of a Gucci store and a colorful fashion spread, suggesting a historical reflection on the brand’s legacy.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a sepia-toned archival shot of a Gucci store facade, establishing a vintage tone, followed by a black-and-white photo of a man in a suit. A woman with short red hair and glasses, seated in a modern, well-lit room, speaks thoughtfully in a clear British accent, explaining that a brand like Gucci, being a hundred years old, needs "fresh input and fresh eyes." Subtle, atmospheric background music begins as the scene shifts to a black-and-white archival photo of two men in suits at a formal event, then a color photo of a man in a dark suit beside a classical statue. The woman's voice continues, noting that while Maurizio Gucci wasn't ultimately successful, he laid groundwork, hiring American retail executive Dawn Mello, who then hired Tom Ford. A graphic overlay introduces Maurizio Gucci, Dawn Mello (EVP & Chief Designer 1989), and Tom Ford (Junior Womenswear Designer 1990) against a repeating Gucci logo pattern. The music continues as an older man with graying hair and a beard, identified as Domenico De Sole, is shown in a color photo and then in an interview in a modern office. He speaks with a deeper, gravelly voice and a distinct accent, explaining that after Maurizio left, he gained more responsibility, becoming the Chief Operating Officer and effectively running the company. He recounts being asked who should be the creative director and stating he "felt very strongly it to be Tom." The graphic reappears, highlighting Tom Ford’s promotion to Creative Director, before concluding with a close-up of Domenico De Sole, emphasizing his reflective expression.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a black-and-white graphic detailing Tom Ford's 1994 promotion to Creative Director under Dawn Mello, as Domenico De Sole's distinct, slightly accented voice begins recounting his vivid memory of driving to Milan and meeting a "very, very young" Tom. The scene transitions to a modern, minimalist interview setting where Sara Forden, with short red hair and glasses, sits opposite Domenico De Sole, who continues his narration, explaining how he told Tom Ford they could "really fix this company." Domenico De Sole, seen speaking in close-up, elaborates on his conversation with Tom, stating he wasn't interested in interfering as a businessman and told Tom, "you're in charge, you know, you do it and let's see where we go here." He concludes, noting Tom "accepted, and that's really the beginning of the story," as a subtle, rhythmic electronic beat begins to fade in. The graphic reappears, now highlighting the 'Tom-Dom' alliance formed in 1999, accompanied by a more prominent electronic beat and the start of a female voiceover. Archival fashion footage shows a model in a black velvet jacket walking a dark runway, while the female voiceover praises Tom Ford for buying back the logo and making it "chic." This is followed by a model in a blue striped shirt and red skirt posing with a Gucci bag against a tropical backdrop, as the voiceover declares, "He makes clothes that rock the world," and asserts he "puts sex back where it belongs." The scene shifts to the VH1 Fashion Awards 1999, showing Jennifer Lopez in a shimmering white dress speaking into a microphone, as the female voiceover introduces, "Here's Tom Ford for Gucci," followed by applause and cheering. Tom Ford, identified as Gucci's Creative Director, then accepts an award, expressing gratitude with his own clear voice, "Thank you. I'd like to thank Vogue. Thank you," amidst continued applause and cheering. The segment cuts to a close-up of a model in a blue striped shirt with bold eye makeup, as the electronic beat fades out and Sara Forden's clear, analytical voice begins, stating, "It's difficult to understate the impact of Tom Ford on Gucci." This is followed by a brief shot of a woman in a floral dress touching her hair, while a different male voice interjects, "No, it's not just about fashion, it's also about real life." Sara Forden's voice returns, describing Tom Ford's "extraordinary style, his innovation, and actually his sense of control."

Domenico De Sole, an older man with graying hair and a beard, then speaks thoughtfully in a softly lit room, his clear, slightly accented voice confirming that 'the sales went through the roof' and that 'investors wanted to go public as fast as possible.' She continues, emphasizing with animated hand gestures, that 'Domenico's being given presentation workshops' and that they 'really pioneered the luxury sector in the financial market,' with other brands 'subsequently follow[ing].' A CNN news clip of a crowded Gucci store and a man being interviewed further illustrates the public and financial attention. Throughout, a light, suspenseful synth background music underscores the narrative.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of Domenico De Sole, an older man with graying hair and a beard, wearing a dark suit, speaking thoughtfully in a softly lit room. He articulates concerns about competitors who "were already in that market" having a "competitive advantage" and the risk that "somebody could take it over given this massive success," noting that "there are always moments in which the stock may go down." The scene then transitions to an exterior shot of a grand Gucci building in Florence, prominently displaying its sign next to a romantic billboard, followed by a bustling Florentine street with market stalls selling leather goods, where a woman in a black zippered jacket browses handbags. A shift to a nighttime Japanese cityscape in 1997, illuminated by vibrant neon signs like '大関' and 'BENKARIS', shows cars and crowds moving through the streets, as a female narrator explains that "In 1997, Gucci was a high-performance machine, but a financial crisis hit Asia, putting Gucci and the rest of the luxury goods market in peril." Domenico De Sole's voice returns, explaining that "Japan was the dominant force in the luxury market at the time," and when the crisis hit Asia, "the stock went down," leading to LVMH starting "purchasing shares of the company," which was his "concern about the possibility [of being] open to a takeover." The video returns to an interview setting, showing Domenico De Sole seated in a white armchair, conversing with Sara Forden, a woman with short red hair and glasses, wearing a black leather jacket, holding papers, in a minimalist, modern room. As rhythmic electronic music plays, Sara Forden asks about "the real sort of rival on the market for Gucci." The segment concludes with a black-and-white graphic displaying portraits of Domenico De Sole and Bernard Arnault, labeled as 'Chairman and Chief Executive LVMH', against a dark background with a repeating Gucci logo pattern.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a black-and-white portrait of Bernard Arnault, labeled 'Chairman and Chief Executive LVMH', set against a Gucci logo pattern, as a female voice (later identified as Sara Forden) describes him as a 'corporate raider in the European luxury scene' for LVMH, having 'taken over Louis Vuitton, Moët & Chandon, and had other fashion brands, he had Dior.' This transitions to archival footage of a Louis Vuitton store ribbon-cutting ceremony, where Bernard Arnault, alongside a woman in a black leather jacket and another in a brown dress, cuts a 'LOUIS VUITTON' ribbon. The scene shifts to a press conference where Bernard Arnault is seated with other men, and a male voice with a French accent, presumably Arnault, articulates their business philosophy: 'We try to build a large business with our partners with one criteria: the best quality and the most elitist product in every line that we are selling throughout the world.' Background music with a rhythmic beat accompanies a shot of Bernard Arnault and a woman in a light gray suit watching a fashion show. A close-up of Bernard Arnault speaking in an interview is shown, as a different male voice with an American accent (later identified as Domenico De Sole) describes receiving a call from Yves Carcelle, CEO of Louis Vuitton, informing him that LVMH had purchased 5% of the company, triggering a public disclosure requirement and marking 'the beginning of the battle with LVMH.' A black Mercedes-Benz drives past on a street, followed by a woman in a white dress and a man in a blue shirt walking outside a store, as Sara Forden asks, 'And did he say something about how it was a friendly acquisition?' A close-up of a 'Louis Vuitton' sign on a building facade is shown, and Domenico De Sole responds, 'Yeah, he said it was very, very friendly.' The segment concludes with an interview featuring Sara Forden, a woman with short red hair and glasses in a black leather jacket, holding papers, and Domenico De Sole, an older man with graying hair and a beard in a dark suit, speaking thoughtfully and gesturing, as Sara smiles and nods, visually confirming the speakers.

The video then cuts to a wide shot of a busy New York City street with yellow taxis and pedestrians, flags fluttering above. The scene shifts to a woman with short red hair and glasses, wearing a black sweater, seated in a bright, cozy room, speaking expressively with red-painted nails. The video then shows a man in a dark suit on a green bench, holding a piece of paper, followed by two men in suits seated at a table, looking at documents. An unidentified assertive male voice-over describes a competitor as "our number one competitor," explaining, "We compete against them for customers, we compete for talent people. This is a case of creeping control. They would like to control the company without paying the full price." The woman with red hair and glasses reappears, gesturing as she speaks, her voice continuing to discuss "The..." The scene transitions to a dark, grainy archival clip of a man with a camera, followed by a close-up of a woman with short dark hair and bold blue eye makeup.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a woman in a black sweater, glasses, and red nails, speaking expressively from a cozy room, narrating the escalating "acrimonious" contest between two companies. She explains how De Sole, with advisors, implemented a "protection mechanism" using employee shares to dilute Arnault's stake. The visual briefly shifts to a man in a dark pinstripe suit before Gucci handbags, then returns to the woman as she continues, stating De Sole realized they couldn't "fend off Arnault forever." As she describes the "ninth hour" intervention, the visuals show a vintage 1980s photograph of two men in suits, followed by a portrait of a smiling man in a dark suit, and another vintage photo of two smiling men at a social event. This leads to a black-and-white graphic of François Pinault, identified as the head of PPR, who "purchases 42% of shares to become majority shareholder" by offering to buy a stake in Gucci, becoming their "white knight" against Arnault. The scene transitions to a modern interview where Sara Forden, in a black leather jacket, sits opposite Domenico De Sole. In a close-up, De Sole, with a distinct voice, gestures as he recounts his conversation with François, whom he calls "the most decisive man in the history of the universe," explaining how he told his story and why they needed to "defend against Arnault." Finally, grainy archival footage from 1999 shows Domenico De Sole walking and smiling with others, a 'CNN NEWSSTAND' logo visible, followed by a group of men in suits moving through a dimly lit building. Over this, a different woman's voice, sounding like a news reporter, reveals that De Sole "drove a hard bargain, securing 3 billion for Gucci's immediate growth," a deal "Bernard Arnault rejected."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a black-and-white archival shot of François Pinault, identified as the Founder of Kering, engaged in a serious conversation. A female voice, belonging to the woman with short red hair and glasses, wearing a black sweater, narrates Pinault's ambition: 'So François Pinault does not only want to be the white knight, but he has an even bigger idea.' The scene transitions to this woman, speaking expressively in a modern interview setting, explaining how Pinault 'turns Yves Saint Laurent $3 billion over to Tom and Dom' with the goal to 'buy up other brands and turn Gucci into a multi-brand group that can rival Arnault and LVMH.' The video then cuts to a vibrant event where Domenico De Sole, an older man with graying hair and a beard, shares a joyful, laughing moment. His distinct male voice with an accent begins, describing the process as 'pretty brutal because we were under pressure to do it, okay? So just it's a lot of work.' As a close-up of a man in a pinstripe suit smiling subtly appears, De Sole continues, 'there are a lot of potential targets. And every time we try to buy something, obviously LVMH would show up.' The scene shifts to a wide interview shot of the woman in the black sweater and Domenico De Sole conversing, followed by archival footage of a fashion show audience. De Sole's voice continues, 'It was not an easy thing to do. So everything had to be done in great secrecy. It was a huge amount of pressure because it was on one end running the company, and on the other hand acquiring all these companies.' A close-up of De Sole speaking thoughtfully is shown, then the woman, now in a black leather jacket, listens intently. De Sole, animated, concludes, 'And we acquired a lot of companies very fast.' Upbeat, rhythmic electronic music begins as the video shows a grainy, vintage fashion show clip with a model and Tom Ford confidently walking the runway. The female voice returns over the music, stating, 'To not only turn around Gucci or keep the Gucci turn...'

This transitions to a woman with short red hair and glasses, wearing a black sweater, speaking expressively in a cozy room, detailing how 'they were both working 24/7,' with Tom Ford designing for both Gucci and Yves Saint Laurent, while also nurturing other designers. The female speaker lists Alexander McQueen, Stella McCartney, and Balenciaga, noting how some were young designers needing help to grow, while others were 'faded names that needed to be turned around,' adding that 'they had a lot of children that they were trying to raise.' A woman in a dark blazer walks with models on a runway, followed by models in bold red and leopard-print outfits posing in front of a 'BALENCIAGA' backdrop. The male voice returns, discussing 'the agreement' to run the company independently for five years and create these 'multi-brand companies.' segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a woman in a white top and light jeans briskly walking down a stone-paved street, carrying multiple colorful shopping bags and holding a phone, embodying a modern consumerist lifestyle. A subtle, dramatic underscore accompanies an older man with graying hair and a beard, wearing a dark suit, who speaks thoughtfully in a softly lit room, explaining the acquisition of 100% of shares at a price set during a battle with LVMH. The scene transitions to a black-and-white graphic of François Pinault, labeled 'Founder, Kering,' detailing his acquisition of 42% of Gucci shares in 1999 and controlling almost all by 2000. A woman with short red hair and glasses, wearing a black sweater, then narrates in a clear, informative voice from a bright, cozy room, explaining that five years after the initial 'white knight' move, Pinault bought the full 100% of Gucci. She describes how Tom Ford and Domenico De Sole were running the publicly traded company as their own until Pinault took control, necessitating contract renegotiations. Realizing they wouldn't have the freedom to run Gucci as before, Tom and Dom announced their resignation, which was a shock to the industry. As the narrative shifts to archival Gucci fashion show footage, featuring models in a beige suit and a black velvet jacket on a runway under the 'GUCCI' logo, the background music becomes more upbeat and celebratory. A graphic then highlights the 'Tom-Dom' alliance formed in 1994, showing portraits of Tom Ford and Domenico De Sole, noting their resignation in 2004, followed by a 'STYLE WARS' magazine cover featuring them. The segment culminates with archival footage of Tom Ford walking confidently down a runway in a dark suit, receiving applause, his close-up illuminated by a spotlight as confetti falls around him, all set to the continuing upbeat music. The older man's calm voice returns, stating that Tom was clear he was not going to stay.

The scene shifts to modern retail, showcasing a light blue double-breasted suit on a mannequin in a Gucci store window, followed by the iconic Gucci store entrance and a Chanel storefront where masked people enter. The older man returns, passionately stating that "Gucci is my legacy" and expressing happiness for its success, adding, "I always want Gucci to do well." Upbeat electronic music begins as the video transitions to a fashion show featuring models in avant-garde outfits, including a blue furry mask and a white hooded ensemble with oversized sunglasses, walking under dramatic lighting. The woman with red hair continues, describing Gucci's "genius" in its ability to "reinvent itself," as a model in a colorful, musical-note-embellished jacket confidently concludes the runway walk.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a dynamic fashion runway sequence, showcasing models in avant-garde Gucci ensembles under dramatic, dark lighting, accompanied by a rhythmic, slightly electronic background track. A model in a colorful, floral-patterned coat with a bejeweled hood walks confidently, followed by another in a sheer black tank top and bright yellow underwear. The scene transitions to Rita Clifton, a brand expert and former chairman of Interbrand UK, with shoulder-length light brown hair and a black zippered jacket, seated in front of a garden view. She articulately explains how Gucci has reinvented 'the sex of its times,' noting that 'sex sells' was crucial to Gucci's rescue in the 1990s. As the video cuts back to the runway, featuring a model in a black velvet dress with a dramatic ruffled collar and then a model in a light blue Gucci tracksuit, Rita continues, highlighting Gucci's recent 'phenomenally successful' years. She elaborates that Gucci has expressed 'sex in a rather different way,' embracing 'gender-blending sex, a non-binary type of sex' that has resonated with millennials and consumers under 35. A close-up of a model in a black leather jacket with red trim and a chain-strap bag appears, overlaid with a Gucci Instagram post detailing the #GucciLoveParade campaign featuring celebrities like Miley Cyrus and Snoop Dogg. The sequence continues with a model in a green leather dress walking down a blue-lit runway, followed by a model in a white, fringed gown with intricate silver embellishments, as Rita concludes that this challenging approach has led to a 'truly runaway and runway success.' The minute concludes with a black screen displaying Bloomberg Businessweek credits, with the background music continuing as the primary sound.
